categories.title: Basics
categories.description: Start here if you're new to meditation

items.title: What Is Meditation?
items.summary: A simple practice of training attention and awareness.

body.text: Meditation is the practice of intentionally directing your attention. That's it. You're not trying to empty your mind, achieve a special state, or stop thinking. You're learning to notice where your attention goes, and to bring it back when it wanders.

Most styles of meditation share the same core mechanic: pick something to pay attention to (the breath, a sound, body sensations), notice when your mind drifts, return without judgment. Each return is a rep. The practice is the returning.
Why people meditate
Calmer reactions to stress
Better focus and concentration
More awareness of thoughts and feelings without being controlled by them
Improved sleep
These benefits are well-documented, but they come from consistency, not intensity. Five minutes a day beats an hour once a week.

items.title: Posture and Setup
items.summary: How to sit, where to sit, what to do with your hands.

body.text: Posture
Sit upright but relaxed. You can use a chair, a cushion on the floor, or even lie down if you're not sleepy. The key is a posture you can maintain comfortably for the duration.
Spine tall, not stiff
Shoulders relaxed, not slumped
Hands resting in your lap or on your knees
Eyes closed, or softly open looking down at the floor
Setup
Pick a quiet spot. Put your phone on do-not-disturb. Set a timer so you don't keep checking the clock. That's it — you don't need anything else to start.
If you've never meditated, start with five minutes. Here's a structure that works for almost anyone:
Sit comfortably and set a 5-minute timer.
Take three slow breaths. Let each exhale be a little longer than the inhale.
Now breathe normally. Pay attention to the sensation of the breath — at the nostrils, or in the belly. Pick one spot and stay there.
When your mind wanders (it will, often), notice that it wandered, and gently bring attention back to the breath.
When the timer goes, take one more deep breath and open your eyes.
That's a complete meditation. The wandering and returning IS the practice — not a sign you're doing it wrong.

items.title: Breath Awareness
items.summary: The most accessible technique. Anchor attention to the breath.

body.text: Breath awareness is the foundation of most meditation traditions. The breath is always with you, it's neutral, and watching it naturally calms the nervous system.
How to do it
Pick a spot to feel the breath: nostrils, chest, or belly.
Breathe naturally. Don't force a rhythm.
When you notice you've drifted, label it gently ('thinking') and return to the breath.
Best for: reducing anxiety, building focus, daily practice.

items.title: Body Scan
items.summary: Move attention slowly through the body.

body.text: A body scan moves your attention systematically from one body part to the next, noticing sensations without trying to change them.
How to do it
Lie down or sit comfortably.
Start at the feet. Notice any sensations — warmth, pressure, tingling, nothing.
Slowly move up: ankles, calves, knees, thighs, hips, belly, chest, hands, arms, shoulders, neck, face, scalp.
Spend 30 seconds to a minute on each region.
Best for: releasing physical tension, falling asleep, reconnecting with the body after a stressful day.

items.title: Loving-Kindness (Metta)
items.summary: Direct warmth toward yourself and others.

body.text: Loving-kindness meditation cultivates a sense of goodwill — toward yourself, then expanding outward. It sounds soft but the research on it is strong, especially for low mood and self-criticism.
How to do it
Bring to mind someone easy to feel warmth for — a friend, a pet, a child.
Silently repeat: 'May you be happy. May you be safe. May you be at ease.'
After a few minutes, direct the same wishes to yourself.
Then to a neutral person, someone difficult, and finally all beings.
Best for: low mood, self-criticism, repairing strained relationships.

Noting is a technique where you give a quiet mental label to whatever shows up in your experience — 'thinking', 'planning', 'remembering', 'hearing', 'feeling'. The label creates a small gap between you and the experience.
This works well if you find pure breath awareness too still — noting gives the busy mind something useful to do.
How to do it
Sit and breathe normally.
When something dominant arises (a thought, a sound, a feeling), label it with one or two words.
Then return to the breath until the next thing arises.
Best for: people whose minds are very active, anxiety, gaining insight into mental patterns.
Regular meditation reduces cortisol levels, slows the heart rate, and dampens the amygdala's reactivity to threat. In plain terms: you become less easily triggered.
This isn't about feeling 'zen' all the time. It's about noticing the moment between stimulus and reaction — and being able to choose the reaction instead of being hijacked by it.
Effective dose: 10–20 minutes a day, most days, for 8 weeks before noticeable change
Even 5 minutes acutely lowers stress in that moment

items.title: Focus and Attention
items.summary: Practice strengthens the mental muscle of returning to task.

body.text: Every time you notice you've drifted in meditation and return to the breath, you're literally training the part of the brain (anterior cingulate cortex) that catches mind-wandering. Over time, this skill carries over to other tasks.
People who meditate consistently report being able to stay on task longer, get back to work faster after a distraction, and feel less mental fatigue.

items.title: Emotional Regulation
items.summary: More space between feeling and acting on the feeling.

body.text: One of the most underrated benefits: you stop feeling like your emotions are commands. Anger arises, you notice it, you let it pass without sending the email. Sadness arises, you sit with it instead of numbing.
This is what people mean by 'mindfulness' — not the absence of difficult emotions, but a different relationship to them.

items.title: Sleep
items.summary: Easier to fall asleep, less middle-of-the-night rumination.

body.text: Meditation before bed (especially body scan or breath awareness) reduces sleep latency — the time it takes to fall asleep — and tends to reduce 3am rumination wake-ups.
The mechanism is simple: you're practicing letting thoughts go without engaging them. That same skill is what lets you fall asleep instead of replaying tomorrow's meeting.
